items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Partly successful: the elevated/mock-heroic opener is flattened into a casual idiom (“nagging feeling”), weakening the comic high-register self-dramatization. More importantly, the second clause is mistranslated as possession (“has”) rather than loss/association (“Shpak—tape recorder; ambassador—medallion”), which the reference notes is crucial: the joke is Bunsha connecting two missing items across eras. This shifts the logic and dulls the callback.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the core comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness and the absurd insistence on “Tsar” as if it were a social introduction. It’s not identical to the reference (which leans into “I’m the Tsar”), but it achieves the same effect of Soviet-formula greeting in a royal context. No meaningful loss of humor or characterization.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and fully preserves the comic authoritarian grandiosity.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Very close and keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than “Leave me,” but the line still reads elevated enough in context and retains the humor of calling her “old woman.”

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is mostly conveyed (being sealed in by “demons”), but the English is unnaturally clipped and loses the subject/voice (“They’ve walled me in”), which is part of the melodramatic complaint. “Bricked up” reads like a fragment and can momentarily confuse (bricked up what? who did it?), reducing comedic clarity.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and functional. Slightly less mock-biblical/ceremonial than “Behold the power…”, but still clearly attributes the ‘miracle’ to the cross and keeps the comic religiosity in a modern setting.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the basic directive but loses the specific emcee/announcer flavor and the sense of opening the dance floor as a formal proclamation. “Everybody dance!” is more like a simple command and less like a hosted event cue, slightly weakening the comic ‘Soviet party host’ vibe described in the reference.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Good creative equivalent: keeps the self-satisfied, opportunistic cheer of the burglar. Slightly more time-focused than luck-focused, but the pragmatic function (delighted appraisal of his own timing/fortune) is preserved and reads idiomatic.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar!

Imported caviar—eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key creative constraint in the reference is not to call the eggplant spread “caviar,” because English viewers will take it literally and miss the Soviet-status inversion joke. The actual translation explicitly says “Imported caviar—eggplant!”, which is exactly the pitfall: it misleads rather than clarifies the gag. Also loses the comic beat of “Да!” and the reveal structure (“overseas … eggplant!”).

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Works well: preserves the escalating inventory rhythm and the comedic afterthought “—three” attached to the suede jacket. “Domestic cigarette cases” is a good rendering of “отечественных.” Minor formatting/line-break choices don’t harm the joke.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Faithful and keeps the calm, rhythmic resignation. Exclamation marks add a bit more punch than the reference’s ellipses, but the comedic effect (shared ‘we’re all crazy’ acceptance) remains intact.

summary.interpretation: 3.18 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.64 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
